retrieved_docs.title: Vasovagal Episode -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The efferent limb of the reflex arc is better understood. Increased vagal firing (increased parasympathetic activity) at the sinus node and the atrioventricular node causes a decrease in heart rate. This decrease in heart rate can be profound, with asystole that can be several seconds long. At the same time, decreased sympathetic activity results in decreased vascular tone in both arterioles and venules. The results in decreased preload, venous return, and ventricular volume. Since cardiac output is the product of stroke volume and heart rate, this reflex arc affects both factors in the equation: slowing the heart rate and decreasing the amount of volume. This results in a drop in the patient's mean arterial pressure. Cerebral autoregulation results in constant cerebral blood flow over a wide range of mean arterial pressures, but when the mean arterial pressure falls below the body's ability to autoregulate, the patient loses consciousness. Typically, if the patient falls or is laid supine, the increase in circulating blood volume from the lower extremities combined with the decreased work necessary to get blood to the brain (i.e., the heart does not have to pump "uphill" against gravity) will cause the patient to regain consciousness rapidly.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthostatic Hypotension -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Upon standing from a supine position, approximately 300 to 800 mL of blood pools in the lower extremities due to gravitational forces, as venous capacitance in the lower extremities is significant. This pooling leads to decreased venous return to the heart and a subsequent reduction in cardiac output, as described by the Frank-Starling Curve. The human body normally compensates through increased sympathetic tone, which constricts arterioles and venous splanchnic vessels, and decreased vagal tone, a response known as the baroreceptor reflex. This increase in sympathetic outflow raises peripheral vascular resistance, helping maintain blood pressure despite reduced cardiac output, as: Mean arterial pressure = Cardiac output × Systemic vascular resistance.

retrieved_docs.title: Venous Insufficiency -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The peripheral venous system serves as a blood reserve and a channel for returning blood to the heart. The patency of blood veins, valves, and muscle pumps is necessary to function the venous system properly. To return to the central circulation, blood must travel against gravity and other pressures in the upright position. The veins of the lower extremities are classified as superficial (above the facial muscle layer), deep (below the fascial layer), and perforator. [13] The superficial venous system consists of the great saphenous vein (GSV), small saphenous vein, and several accessory veins. Deep veins are made up of axial veins. Perforating veins traverse the fascial layer, connecting superficially to deep veins. A number of one-way bicuspid valves throughout the deep and superficial veins allow blood to travel toward the heart while preventing it from returning to the feet. [14] Chronic venous insufficiency pathophysiology is either due to reflux (ie, backward flow) or obstruction of venous blood flow. Chronic venous insufficiency can develop from the protracted valvular incompetence of superficial, deep, or perforating veins that connect them. In all cases, the result is venous hypertension of the lower extremities. The resting venous pressure is a summation of the outflow obstruction, capillary inflow, valve function, and muscle pump function.

retrieved_docs.title: Orthostasis (Archived) -- Introduction -- Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: When one stands up, gravity causes a shift of 500 to 800 mL of blood volume from the upper body to the lower body and the splanchnic circulation. This redistribution of blood decreases the venous return and the right atrium’s pressure, reducing the stroke volume and eventually a fall in arterial blood pressure. [1] For this change in position, the body has 4 compensatory mechanisms: Irregular distribution of blood in the venous system (there are pools of blood located in the central circulation, that can be used when hypotension is detected) Non-uniform distensibility (small vessels are more rigid than large vessels. Thus, small veins prevent a large accumulation of blood in the lower body and help to maintain a relative upward flow of blood) Muscle pumps (increase the venous return) Autonomic reflexes Out of these, autonomic reflexes are the most important mechanism. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Valsalva Maneuver -- Technique or Treatment -- Modified Valsalva Maneuver
retrieved_docs.content: To increase the relaxation phase venous return and vagal stimulation, a modification to the standard Valsalva maneuver has been described in the REVERT trial, which includes supine positioning with leg elevation immediately after the Valsalva strain. The modified Valsalva maneuver is used for the emergency treatment of supraventricular tachycardia. [14] Use of the modified Valsalva maneuver in postoperative management of chronic subdural hematoma is safe and results in reduced rates of recurrence and infection after a burr hole drainage. [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Phlebotomy -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Simple steps like counting down might significantly decrease anxiety and optimize the condition for proceeding with the venipuncture. In case of severe history of panic attacks during the procedure, the patient might be asked to lie down. Special attention should be paid to the patients' position during the procedure. [22] Statistically significant differences with changing patients' positioning due to several biochemistry parameters have been reported. Therefore position maintenance, whether is lying down or sitting, within at least 10 to 15 minutes of the procedure is recommended. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Blood Vessels -- Introduction -- Veins
retrieved_docs.content: Blood flows from venules into larger veins. Just like the arterial system, three layers make up the vein walls. But unlike the arteries, the venous pressure is low. Veins are thin-walled and are less elastic. This feature permits the veins to hold a very high percentage of the blood in circulation. The venous system can accommodate a large volume of blood at relatively low pressures, a feature termed high capacitance. At any point in time, nearly three-fourths of the circulating blood volume is contained in the venous system. One can also find one-way valves inside veins that allow for blood flow, toward the heart, in a forward direction. Muscle contractions aid the blood flow in the leg veins. The forward blood flow from the lower extremities to the heart is also influenced by respiratory changes that affect pressure gradients in the abdomen and chest cavity. This pressure differential is highest during deep inspiration, but a small pressure differential is observable during the entire respiratory cycle.

retrieved_docs.title: Venous Insufficiency -- History and Physical -- Clinical Features
retrieved_docs.content: The physical exam should involve a detailed assessment of any ulcers, distal pulses, and neuropathy in the upright and supine positions. The Trendelenburg test may help differentiate between the CVI caused by the superficial vein valves and the deep system. The patient's leg is elevated to perform the test, and all the venous blood is emptied. The surgeon then compresses the groin firmly to occlude the greater saphenous vein junction and asks the patient to stand up. If the leg does not fill up with venous blood, this indicates that incompetent valves in the superficial veins cause CVI. If the leg fills with venous blood, the valves connecting the superficial veins to the deep ones are incompetent. The treatment is limited to compression stockings if the deep system valves are involved. [4] [5] [3] [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Valsalva Maneuver -- Anatomy and Physiology
retrieved_docs.content: Phase II : Phase II is divided into early (PII E ) and late (PII L ) phases. Phase PII E starts when positive intrathoracic pressure reduces venous return to the heart. Because of reduced venous return and thus reduced preload, stroke volume falls; this leads to a fall in blood pressure. [6] This fall in blood pressure activates the baroreceptors in the carotid sinus and aortic arch, marking the beginning of Phase PII L . The vagal withdrawal, followed by increased sympathetic discharge, ensues, leading to marked tachycardia, increased cardiac output, and vasoconstriction, which leads to the recovery of blood pressure to normal values in healthy individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Uterus -- Mechanism -- Pregnancy
retrieved_docs.content: The size of the uterus leads to additional changes throughout the body. When lying down, the uterus can compress the inferior vena cava, causing decreased blood flow to the right atrium and hypotension. The uterus also presses on the pelvic veins, leading to lower leg swelling and varicose veins.

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Patient Positioning -- Structure and Function -- Supine Position Variations
retrieved_docs.content: Trendelenburg position: A variation of supine in which the head of the bed is tilted down such that the pubic symphysis is the highest point of the trunk facilitates venous return and improves exposure during abdominal and laparoscopic surgeries. Hemodynamic changes, including increased venous return and cardiac output, are temporary, with most hemodynamic variables returning to baseline within ten minutes. Respiratory changes, including upward displacement of the abdominal contents into the diaphragm, decrease functional residual capacity and respiratory compliance, requiring higher airway pressures to maintain ventilation. Gravitational changes from prolonged head-down positioning can increase intracranial pressure, intraocular pressure, and swelling of the face, larynx, and tongue, increasing the risk for postoperative airway obstruction. Sliding and shifting of a patient in Trendelenburg positioning are often prevented with shoulder braces. However, caution must be used to prevent undue pressure, potentially resulting in compression or stretch injury to the brachial plexus.

retrieved_docs.title: Nocturia -- Etiology -- Other Causes and Contributing Factors
retrieved_docs.content: Peripheral edema can contribute to nocturnal polyuria. When a patient with significant lower extremity edema lies down, much of the extra fluid is returned to the vascular system, where the kidneys can excrete it. This would increase urine production shortly after the patient assumes a recumbent or supine position. This effect can be minimized by having the patient elevate their lower extremities sometime before bed, as well as the judicious use and timing of diuretics. Similarly, heart failure, nephrotic syndrome, venous insufficiency, high sodium intake, and even just long-term standing still may also cause excess fluid retention in the lower extremities, which are mobilized once the patient assumes recumbency, resulting in increased urine production shortly after the patient lies down. [3] Compression stockings during the daytime, especially in the afternoon and evenings, can also help minimize tissue fluid sequestration in the lower extremities before bedtime. [3] [4] Drinking large amounts of fluids shortly before going to bed and ingesting caffeine or alcohol late in the day and before bed is likely to contribute to nocturia as well. [3] [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Autonomic Dysfunction -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Orthostatic hypotension is defined as a sustained reduction of systolic blood pressure of at least 20 mmHg or diastolic blood pressure of 10 mmHg within three minutes of standing or head-up tilt to at least 60 degrees on the tilt table. The magnitude of fall in blood pressure depends on baseline blood pressure. In patients with supine hypertension, a reduction of 30 mmHg in systolic blood pressure may be an appropriate criterion for orthostatic hypotension. Immediately upon standing, gravitationally mediated redistribution of the blood volume and pooling of 300 to 800 ml of blood in lower limbs and splanchnic venous capacitance system occurs, which leads to a reduction of venous return and cardiac filling pressure. During standing, contraction of skeletal muscles of the lower body prevents excessive pooling and increases venous return to the heart. Orthostatic hypotension is caused by an excessive fall of cardiac output or by inadequate or defective vasoconstrictor mechanisms.

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Autonomic Nervous System -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Orthostatic intolerance (OI) is a condition characterized by the onset of symptoms on standing that improve by lying down. An upright position is a stressor on the circulatory system that requires a healthy functioning heart, adequate blood volume, and functioning vascular tone to maintain appropriate blood pressures and cerebral blood flow. Without any one of these factors, symptoms of dizziness and loss of consciousness can occur on standing. The sympathetic nervous system exerts its influence to increase heart rate and cause vasoconstriction and venoconstriction. Without this feature of the sympathetic nervous system, OI will occur. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Central Venous Pressure -- Mechanism
retrieved_docs.content: Theoretically, when the mean systemic filling pressure equals the central venous pressure, there will be no venous return. The CVP is inversely related to venous return. However, another factor to consider is intrathoracic pressure. If the central venous pressure were to fall below the intrathoracic pressure, the central veins become compressed and limit venous return. The peripheral venous pressure can be affected by a change in volume, and because of their compliant nature, a change in total volume would have a greater effect on the amount of blood present in the veins. The venous tone is regulated by the sympathetic nervous system as well as external compression forces. Under normal physiologic conditions, the right and left ventricular output are equal. The central venous pressure influences cardiac (left ventricle) output - this is driven by changes in central venous pressure which lead to changes in the filling pressures of the left heart.

retrieved_docs.title: Aortocaval Compression Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The lordotic curvature of the lumbar spine causes an outward bowing of the vertebral column in the lower (L4 - L5) spinal area towards the abdominal-pelvic cavity. The uterus is located intra-abdominally at the same L4 – L5 vertebral level.  Juxtaposed between the lumbar spine and the uterus is the inferior vena cava. While gravity works to keep the uterus and enclosed fetus outward and off of the inferior vena cava and lumbar spine when the mother is upright or sitting, that is not the case when in the supine position. Once in the supine position, gravity allows the uterus to rest posteriorly onto the lordotic spine, potentially compressing the inferior vena cava under its weight. As a result of this compression, the blood flow returning from the extremities may be impeded drastically, resulting in maternal hypotension.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2651
retrieved_docs.content: Varicose vein ligation and stripping directly targets the vein (vény), reflecting that this surgical procedure is performed on the vascular structures themselves. The vein (vény) also has an indirect procedural association with blood flow (إراقة الدم), underscoring its essential role in circulating blood throughout the body. This relationship highlights how the treatment of varicose veins is intrinsically linked to both the anatomical site of the veins and their functional involvement in blood movement.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: Operative procedures on vein are performed at the anatomical site identified as “vény,” which denotes a vein. The specific surgical technique of varicose vein ligation and stripping directly targets the “vény,” indicating that this procedure is carried out on the vein itself. In addition to serving as the direct site for such operative interventions, the “vény” also functions as an indirect procedural site related to “إراقة الدم,” the process of blood drainage, reflecting the vein’s role in facilitating the movement of blood out of tissues. Together, these relationships highlight the central importance of the vein (“vény”) both as a direct target for surgical treatment of varicose veins and as an indirect conduit for blood flow management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2158
retrieved_docs.content: Varicose vein ligation and stripping is a surgical technique that is performed directly on veins, specifically targeting the abnormal, dilated vessels known as varicose veins. The procedure involves ligating (tying off) the affected vein to prevent blood flow and then stripping (removing) the vein segment, thereby alleviating symptoms such as pain, swelling, and cosmetic concerns. By acting directly on the vein, this intervention addresses the underlying venous insufficiency that leads to varicosities and contributes to improved lower‑extremity circulation and patient quality of life.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 1 -- Community 238
retrieved_docs.content: venous procedures such as varicose‑vein ligation and stripping, and anorectal hemorrhoidal treatments through common procedural categories (incision, excision, occlusion), shared anatomical sites (vein, arterial and venous plexuses), and unified clinical objectives of restoring perfusion, relieving venous congestion, and managing hemorrhoidal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2676
retrieved_docs.content: Stripping of lower limb varicose veins is classified as a vascular surgical procedure on the lower limb, representing a specific operative technique used to treat varicose veins by removing or ligating the diseased superficial veins. This procedure falls within the broader category of lower‑limb vascular surgeries, which encompass various interventions aimed at restoring normal venous and arterial function in the legs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1137
retrieved_docs.content: The comprehensive evaluation of cardiovascular function with tilt table evaluation, which includes continuous ECG monitoring and intermittent blood pressure monitoring and may be performed with or without pharmacological intervention, incorporates ضغط الدم as a fundamental component. In this context, ضغط الدم is directly associated with ضغط الدم المنخفض, emphasizing that monitoring blood pressure during tilt table testing is essential for identifying low blood pressure (hypotension) and guiding appropriate clinical management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 145
retrieved_docs.content: lower‑limb embolectomy/endarterectomy, and varicose vein ligation/stripping—are organized by anatomical site, procedural hierarchy, and functional outcomes such as restoration of blood flow, thereby supporting comprehensive diagnostic, therapeutic, and postoperative patient care across vascular specialties.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 153
retrieved_docs.content: hierarchical family of “Incision, excision, and occlusion of vessels” and is an inverse‑isa (subtype) of varicose vein stripping; lower limb. This operation directly targets the vein (procedure site “vény”), and the vein also has an indirect procedural association with إراقة الدم, indicating that blood drainage may be a secondary effect of venous interventions. Stripping of lower limb varicose veins is likewise an inverse‑isa of both the broader “Vascular surgical procedure on lower limb” and the generic category “varicose vein stripping; lower limb,” reinforcing its placement within lower‑extremity venous surgery. The anatomical substrate for these venous procedures is the Structure of vein of lower extremity, which serves as the direct procedure site for varicose vein stripping; lower limb and is the finding site for varicose veins of lower extremity. Varicose veins of lower extremity are associated morphologically with قَرحَة (ulceration) and are conceptually nested under تدلي

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1232
retrieved_docs.content: Operative procedure on vein is a surgical intervention that is performed directly on the vén (vein), establishing the vein as the primary anatomical site of the operation. In addition, the vein serves as an indirect procedural site for إراقة الدم (blood‑drainage) procedures, indicating that while blood‑drainage interventions may not act directly on the vein, the vessel is nonetheless involved in the pathway of such treatments. This dual role of the vein—as a direct target for operative procedures and an indirect conduit for blood‑drainage techniques—underscores its central importance in vascular interventions and related patient care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 1 -- Community 98
retrieved_docs.content: in turn is a parent of varicose vein ligation and stripping. Varicose vein ligation and stripping is performed on a vein (vény), is indirectly linked to blood flow (إراقة الدم), and is classified as a type of varicose vein stripping; lower limb through an inverse‑is‑a relationship to the broader category lower‑limb varicose vein stripping. The procedure stripping of lower‑limb varicose veins is inversely related both to the general vascular surgical procedure on lower limb and to varicose vein stripping; lower limb, with the structure of vein of lower extremity identified as its direct anatomical site. The specific vascular group embolectomy and endarterectomy of lower limbs covers lower‑extremity arterial interventions; it is a child of operative procedure on artery of extremity and a subset of vascular surgical procedure on lower limb (inverse‑isa). The procedure embolectomy with catheter of femoropopliteal artery by leg incision is an isa instance of this group, illustrating a

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 1 -- Community 240
retrieved_docs.content: of blood drainage (“إراقة الدم”), either directly through arterial clearance or indirectly via venous manipulation. Understanding these hierarchical and relational links—ranging from broad categories like vascular surgical procedures on the lower limb to specific techniques such as catheter‑guided embolectomy of the femoropopliteal artery and varicose vein ligation and stripping—facilitates comprehensive patient assessment, appropriate procedural selection, and coordinated care for arterial occlusive disease and chronic venous insufficiency of the lower extremities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 153
retrieved_docs.content: limb and is the finding site for varicose veins of lower extremity. Varicose veins of lower extremity are associated morphologically with قَرحَة (ulceration) and are conceptually nested under تدلي الأوردة في الاطراف السفلية (vein sagging in the lower limbs), the parent term describing the clinical condition of lower‑extremity varicosities. Together, these relationships delineate a coherent framework in which arterial embolectomy/endarterectomy and venous varicose‑vein ligation/stripping are organized as specialized incisional vascular surgeries on the lower limbs, linked by common procedural themes such as vessel incision, blood drainage, and the anatomical focus on lower‑extremity arteries and veins. Understanding this hierarchy aids clinicians in selecting appropriate surgical techniques, anticipating related outcomes such as ulcer formation, and recognizing the interconnected nature of arterial and venous interventions in lower‑limb vascular care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 1231
retrieved_docs.content: Incision of blood vessel, Incision, excision, and occlusion of vessels, and Varicose vein ligation and stripping are interrelated surgical concepts that form a hierarchical procedural framework. Incision of blood vessel represents a basic operative action that is encompassed within the broader category of Incision, excision, and occlusion of vessels, which includes a range of techniques for accessing, removing, or sealing vascular structures. Within this category, Varicose vein ligation and stripping is a specific procedure that applies the principles of incision, excision, and occlusion to treat incompetent veins. The direct procedural site of Varicose vein ligation and stripping is the vein (vény), meaning the surgery is performed on the venous tissue itself. In addition to this direct target, the procedure has an indirect effect on blood flow (إراقة الدم), as ligating and stripping the vein alters the circulation of blood through the treated vascular segment. Understanding the hierarchical relationship among these procedures and their direct and indirect anatomical targets is essential for clinicians planning vascular interventions and for optimizing patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 899
retrieved_docs.content: Incision of blood vessel is a general surgical concept that includes techniques for cutting, removing, or occluding vascular structures. A more specific subset of these techniques is described as Incision, excision, and occlusion of vessels, which represents the operative actions used in procedures such as Varicose vein ligation and stripping. Varicose vein ligation and stripping is a targeted surgical intervention for treating varicose veins; it is performed directly on veins (referred to as vén) and specifically on the Structure of vein of lower extremity. This procedure is also classified under the broader category of varicose vein stripping; lower limb, indicating that the lower‑limb stripping technique is a particular form of varicose vein ligation and stripping. Together, these relationships illustrate how general vascular incision concepts narrow down to specialized procedures aimed at correcting venous pathology in the lower extremities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2380
retrieved_docs.content: Varicose vein ligation and stripping is a surgical approach that includes the procedures of incision, excision, and occlusion of vessels as its constituent techniques.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2649
retrieved_docs.content: Hemorrhoids is classified as a type of venous disorder (اضطراب الوريد), reflecting its origin in abnormal dilation and dysfunction of the venous vessels of the anorectal region. This relationship underscores that hemorrhoids arise from underlying vascular pathology, linking the clinical presentation of swollen, painful, or bleeding anal cushions directly to disturbances in venous blood flow and vessel integrity. Recognizing hemorrhoids as a manifestation of a broader venous disorder informs both diagnostic evaluation and therapeutic strategies, emphasizing the importance of addressing venous health to manage and prevent hemorrhoidal disease.
